documents.excerpt:
> 1\. ROI selection

ROIs from adjacent t-CyCIF images were selected based on five immune
markers: CD4 (CD4 T cells, but macrophages can also express), CD8 (CD8 T
cells), CD11c (DCs, but not restricted), Iba1 (macrophages) and NKG2a
(NKs). The raw images of lower resolution (ZARR 3) were pre-processed.
Firstly, a binary matrix for each marker was created based on the
percentile threshold depending

protocols.title: Discussion with Iga about Filling the Experimental Planning sheet:
steps: ### word/document.xml 11.6.2024 Discussion with Iga about Filling the Experimental Planning sheet: Sample ID slide will be the first sheet.

It should be machine readable , eg: no spaces, no extra characters no Capital letters.It will have information about the experiment panel that worked : eg: Experiment_1 or Experiment_2 or whichever is the final document.
Experimental Panel sheet named Experiment_X in which all the antibodies worked or the final one should always be the second sheet in our experimental planning.
Experiment_1 is the default name .If we repeat the experiment in case staining fails, we will have subsequent sheets with naming Experiment_2 , Experiment_3 etc.
The antibodies in experiment Panel will have the antibodies unique ID.
Unique antibody ID is given to each new vial even if the catalog number is the same.
However, if catolog number and lot number both are same then even different vial antibodies will have the same unique ID.
Column A named cycle Column C named antibody_id and column E named fluorochrome in Experiment panel sheet must be machine readable.
Information should be uniform, and no extra texts can be written in these columns.
It is researchers responsibility to fill all the columns in Experiment_1 sheet.

> 1\. ROI selection

ROIs from adjacent t-CyCIF images were selected based on five immune
markers: CD4 (CD4 T cells, but macrophages can also express), CD8 (CD8 T
cells), CD11c (DCs, but not restricted), Iba1 (macrophages) and NKG2a
(NKs). The raw images of lower resolution (ZARR 3) were pre-processed.
Firstly, a binary matrix for each marker was created based on the
percentile threshold depending

11.6.2024

Discussion with Iga about Filling the Experimental Planning sheet:

- Sample ID slide will be the first sheet. It should be machine
 > readable , eg: no spaces, no extra characters no Capital
 > letters.It will have information about the experiment panel that
 > worked : eg: Experiment_1 or Experiment_2 or whichever is the
 > final document.

- Experimental Panel sheet named Experiment_X in which all the
 > antibodies worked or the final one should always be the second
 > sheet in our experimental planning. Experiment_1 is the default
 > name .If we repeat the experiment in case staining fails, we will
 > have subsequent sheets with naming Experiment_2 , Experiment_3
 > etc.

- The antibodies in experiment Panel will have the antibodies unique
 > ID.

- Unique antibody ID is given to each new vial even if the catalog
 > number is the same. However, if catolog number and lot number both
 > are same then even different vial antibodies will have the same
 > unique ID.

- Column A named [cycle]{.mark} Column C named [antibody_id]{.mark}
 > and column E named [fluorochrome]{.mark} in Experiment panel sheet
 > must be machine readable. Information should be uniform, and no
 > extra texts can be written in these columns.

- It is researchers responsibility to fill all the columns in

vector_chunks.source_file: 2. Methods and experiments/GeoMx & tCycIF Experiments Methodology.md
vector_chunks.text:
> 1\. ROI selection

ROIs from adjacent t-CyCIF images were selected based on five immune
markers: CD4 (CD4 T cells, but macrophages can also express), CD8 (CD8 T
cells), CD11c (DCs, but not restricted), Iba1 (macrophages) and NKG2a
(NKs). The raw images of lower resolution (ZARR 3) were pre-processed.
Firstly, a binary matrix for each marker was created based on the
percentile threshold depending on the sample. Next, with the help of
morphological operations, small noise was minimised bigger clusters of
signals were created. The following procedure was used:

> 1\) Opening -- kernel 10x10
>
> 2\) Closing -- kernel 20x20
>
> 3\) Dilation -- kernel 20x20

Next, the overlap of pre-processed images between each pair, triplet,
quadruplet and five of markers was found. Those images were then
pre-processed to find bigger connected neighbourhoods, and ten ROIs were
saved. These ROIs were then checked for overlapping labels on cell
interactions. Finally, ROIs were visually checked and picked in NAPARI.
In total, we had ten ROIs per sample in Batch 2 and sample-dependent in
Batch 3; in each sample, we attempted to collect as many diverse ROIs as
possible.

Only NKs cells did not exhibit hub structures. For them, either
artificial or an overlap with Vimentin or PanCK was used.

Details on ROIs can be found here:

vector_chunks.text:
{.underline}](https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1JtjMpnAPUM6-O3FQ39PEuolsvUWy7eFB?usp=sharing)

All experiment procedures, details and corresponding notes can be found
here:

[[GeoMx_P9_EyeMTbatch3_P7_TLS_1.xlsx]{.underline}](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1copNEOCLrFFzoN8L73GupVir-I4lXB7i/edit?gid=1286186248#gid=1286186248)

Formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded (FFPE) tissue sections (5 µm) were
sectioned adjacent to tCycIF slides. These sections are then processed
according to the manufacturer's instructions for the GeoMx Digital
Spatial Profiler (DSP; Bruker Spatial Biology, MAN-10150-06). Briefly,
slides were baked overnight, deparaffinized, and rehydrated before
target retrieval in 1× Tris-EDTA (pH 9.0) using a steamer at \~99 °C,
followed by Proteinase K digestion at 37 °C to expose RNA targets.
Sections were then postfixed and hybridized overnight with the GeoMx RNA
Probe Mix containing UV-cleavable oligonucleotide tags. After two
stringent washes, customized morphology markers and nuclear stains were
applied to enable ROI visualization.

Slides were scanned on the GeoMx DSP instrument to acquire whole slide
fluorescence morphology images, and regions of interest (ROIs) were
selected for illumination. Upon UV exposure, oligonucleotide tags were
released from each ROI and aspirated into a collection plate for library
preparation.

vector_chunks.source_file: 2. Methods and experiments/GeoMx & tCycIF Experiments Methodology.md
vector_chunks.text:
of interest (ROIs) were
selected for illumination. Upon UV exposure, oligonucleotide tags were
released from each ROI and aspirated into a collection plate for library
preparation. Libraries were PCR-amplified using GeoMx Seq Code primers,
pooled, purified, QCed and sequenced on Novaseq X 10B 100c following the
GeoMx NGS Readout User Manual (MAN-10153). FASTQ files were processed
using the GeoMx NGS Pipeline to generate digital count conversion (DCC)
files.

[GeoMx]{.underline}

The GeoMx spatial transcriptomics procedure was conducted at the Single
Cell Analytics unit within the Institute for Molecular Medicine Finland
(FIMM), adhering to the prescribed GeoMx guidelines for slide staining
and scanning. The human Solid Tumor TME morphology marker kit was
employed for this purpose. The tissue slide chosen to perform GeoMx
sequencing was adjacent to the one on which lowplex t-CyCIF have been
performed, to enable guided ROI selection. 

[GeoMx ROI selection with lowplex  t-CyCIF crop overlay]{.underline}

Lowplex t-CyCIF stainings were employed to guide the selection of
regions of interest (ROIs, n=160) in the GeoMx experiment. The chosen
regions contained tumor-stroma interface features, indicated by the
presence of PanCK^+^ and Vimentin^+^ cells, and a variety of CD8 (CD8^+^
T cells) and IBA1 (macrophages) staining markers for optimal ROI
identification within the t-CyCIF crop (CD8^+^IBA1^+^, CD8^+^IBA1^-^,
CD8^-^IBA1^+^, CD8^-^IBA1^-^). The ROIs were selected based on the
relative abundance of CD8^+^ and IBA1^+^ cells observed within a given
slide.  The chosen t-CycIF crop was imported into the GeoMx software and
manually superimposed onto the GeoMx scan using  landmarks present in
both images. Consistency in choosing similar types of areas, avoiding
tissue folds, necrotic regions, or adipose tissue, while maintaining

e GeoMx scan using  landmarks present in
both images. Consistency in choosing similar types of areas, avoiding
tissue folds, necrotic regions, or adipose tissue, while maintaining
similarities in ROI size, tumor-stroma ratio and cell count across all
samples was aimed. Each ROI was segmented into 2 distinct areas of
Illumination (AOIs, n=320) separately for the tumor and stromal
compartments. 

[GeoMx and t-CyCIF data pairing]{.underline}

Whole slide image alignment was achieved in
QuPath[^[100]{.underline}^](https://paperpile.com/c/kKhQh7/nzKZ) through
the Warpy plugin, which performs affine registration, incorporating the
moving image to the coordinate system of the base image. The resulting
files were exported as pyramidal ome.tiff files to be analyzed in other
platforms. 

[GeoMx data quality control and preprocessing.]{.underline}

DCC files obtained from the sequence provider were handled with the
usage of GeomxTools R
package[^[101]{.underline}^](https://paperpile.com/c/kKhQh7/AhRI),
provided by Nanostring. Segment (AOI) and probe level QC as well as LOQ
(limit of quantification) filtering were performed as described in the
package vignette. Overall, [four AOIs]{.mark} and almost 2000 genes were
removed during this procedure, leaving a total number of 310 AOIs and
16,727 genes detected in the whole dataset with sufficient quality. The

Is]{.mark} and almost 2000 genes were
removed during this procedure, leaving a total number of 310 AOIs and
16,727 genes detected in the whole dataset with sufficient quality. The
median number of genes detected per AOI was 7911, and the median
detection rate was 0.42.

[GeoMx normalisation and batch effect correction]{.underline}

We've obtained a GeoMx spatial transcriptomics data from 16 HGSC samples
(10 paired pre-post NACT treatment and 6 unpaired post-NACT treatment),
described in the Launonen et.al 2024 and deposited under
[[https://doi.org/10.7303/syn53283672]{.underline}](https://doi.org/10.7303/syn53283672).
Data contains 160 regions of interest (ROIs) from tumor-stromal
interface, segmented into independent tumor and stromal compartments
(areas of illumination - AOIs, n=320). ROIs have been selected based on
co-occurrence of CD8 positive T-cells and IBA1 positive macrophages. In
this study we've utilized all tumor AOIs (n=160) regardless of presence
or absence of CD8+/IBA1+ cells.

Since the data have been already subjected to the basic quality control,
we performed normalization using Deseq2 followed by a variance
stabilising transformation from the Deseq2 R package. Batch effect
correction has been performed using the harmony R package.

[GeoMx signal deconvolution]{.underline}

Since GeoMx data for each AOI contains a mixture of signals derived from

fect
correction has been performed using the harmony R package.

[GeoMx signal deconvolution]{.underline}

Since GeoMx data for each AOI contains a mixture of signals derived from
each cell type within TME, we performed deconvolution of the signal to
obtain the cell-type specific transcriptional profiles. To do that, we
utilized
BayesPrism[^[55]{.underline}^](https://paperpile.com/c/kKhQh7/xlxP) -
designed for deconvolution of bulk RNAseq data, but validated on various
spatial transcriptomics data, which apart from the fraction of cell
types is able to compute the cell-type specific transcriptomics
profiles. Since this method is based on reference scRNA data, we
utilized the annotated ovarian cancer scRNA-seq dataset published under
GSE266577. We downsampled the full dataset in a patient-specific manner,
generating the final dataset containing 24560 cells and 33458 genes. Due
to the limitations of algorithms to correctly predict fractions of
fine-grained immune cell types, we decided to cluster similar cell types
into more general groups. Finally, as a reference for deconvolution
algorithms, we used scRNA-seq profiles of: 9168 T cells (Regulatory T
cells, Tcm/Naive helper T cells, Tem/Trm cytotoxic T cells, Type 17
helper T cells), 5240 tumor cells, 4364 macrophages (Classical
monocytes, macrophages), 2335 fibroblasts, 1515 B cells (Memory B cells,

T cells, Tem/Trm cytotoxic T cells, Type 17
helper T cells), 5240 tumor cells, 4364 macrophages (Classical
monocytes, macrophages), 2335 fibroblasts, 1515 B cells (Memory B cells,
Naive B cells, Plasma cells), 1007 NK cells (CD16^-^ NK cells, CD16^+^
NK cells, NK cells), 403 Dendritic cells (DC1, DC2, Migratory DCs, pDC),
287 mast cells, 90 endothelial cells and 151 'other' cells (ILC, Late
erythroid cells). 

For BayesPrism method we performed deconvolution according to their
vignette (**see Key resources table**), filtering out low complexity
genes from the downsampled scRNA-seq dataset, using above mentioned cell
groups as 'cell types' and 'cell states' annotation (for cell states,
tumor cells from each patient were annotated separately). As an input we
used raw scRNA-seq and GeoMx counts, as suggested in the vignette.

[GeoMx MHC-II pathway activity analysis and linear
modelling]{.underline}

We've calculated a ssGSEA score of activity of MHC-II gene set (HLA-DMA,
HLA-DMB, HLA-DOA, HLA-DOB, HLA-DPA1, HLA-DPB1, HLA-DQA1, HLA-DQA2,
HLA-DQB1, HLA-DRA, HLA-DRB1, HLA-DRB5, CD74) for the tumor AOIs, on full
as well as tumor-specific de convoluted signal (obtained from
BayesPrism), using
GSVA[^[105]{.underline}^](https://paperpile.com/c/kKhQh7/luue) R
package.

Finally we examined the relation between ssGSEA score for MHC-II gene

(obtained from
BayesPrism), using
GSVA[^[105]{.underline}^](https://paperpile.com/c/kKhQh7/luue) R
package.

Finally we examined the relation between ssGSEA score for MHC-II gene
set and fraction of cell types (obtained by BayesPrism) in the same AOI.
We fitted a basic linear model (y \~ exp(x)), followed by a model with
added 'Patient' as a cofounder factor (y \~ exp(x) \| Patient), given
the fact that AOIs cannot be treated as independent observations.

Macrophages

STROMA: enrichment in:
phagocytosis
cell recognition

TUMOR: enrichment in:
cell division, translation, mitochondrial, energy production
response to stress (heat, chemical, ER), ROS and NOS production, unfolded protein response, autophagy, ER induced apoptosis, mitochondrial proteins degradation
regulation of vehicle transport, endocytosis
proteasome activity, APP on MHC-I, MHC-II and cross-presentation
leukocyte cell-cell adhesion, migration, chemotaxis
response to IFN
negative regulation of immune response, IL10 production

DGE and GO enrichment calculated on cell-type specific deconvoluted transcriptomics profiles, corrected for ct fraction, with sample as cofounder

Macrophages actively infiltrate tumor mass and are engaged in communication with tumor cells (vehicles such as CD9, endocytosis, APP). They get highly activated (division, translation, energy production) through IFN response, but it leads to high cellular stress and ER induced apoptosis/autophagy. Finally their response is reduced (IL-10 production). Similar, but less striking changes have been found in pre_NACT samples, but without APP on MHC.

Differences in phenotype of CD8+ T-cells residing in stroma vs tumor compartments of post-NACT samples

STROMA: enrichment in:
ECM assembly and organisation
T-cell stimulation: activity, cytotoxicity, differentiation, proliferation, chemotaxis by CCL5, LAPTM5, PTPRC
T-cell immunosuppresive regulation by TGFb and APOE
APP on MHC-II (?)
increased immunoglobulin recombination and production (?)

CD8+ T-cells

DGE and GO enrichment calculated on cell-type specific deconvoluted transcriptomics profiles, corrected for ct fraction, with sample as cofounder

TUMOR: slight enrichment in:
translation, energy production
response to stress (heat, chemical), mitochondrial proteins degradation, NO production
proteasome activity
homotypic/cell-cell adhesion

main T-cell activity takes place in stroma, regulated in a complex manner (both pos and neg) by CCL5, LAPTM5, TGFb, and PTPRC. Stromal CD8+ Tcells have increased immunoglobulin recombination and production (TCR?), expression of MHCII molecules and are changing ECM through COL production mediated by TGFB. In contrary, pre_NACT stromal CD8+ T-cell are enriched in immunosuppressive signalling through TGFb and IL10RA

Differences in phenotype of DCs residing in stroma vs tumor compartments of post-NACT samples

STROMA: enrichment in:
actine organisation
ECM organisation
regulation of IL-12, IL2, TNFa, TGFb production
regulation (pos/neg) of immune functions mediated by LAPTM5, PTPRC, TYROBP, CCR7, CST7, IL7R
high activation and regulation of MHC-II APP

DCs

DGE and GO enrichment calculated on cell-type specific deconvoluted transcriptomics profiles, corrected for ct fraction, with sample as cofounder

TUMOR: slight enrichment in:
chaperone mediated protein folding
response to stress, autophagy mitochondrial proteins degradation
proteasome activity, APP 
response to IFN

main DCs activity happens in stroma, regulated in a complex manner (both pos and neg) by LAPTM5, PTPRC, TYROBP, CCR7, CST7, IL7R, resulting in regulation of IL12, IL2, TNFa and TGFb production. Stromal DCs have much higher activity of MHC-II mediated APP, and actively changes ECM. Tumor infiltrated DCs again are showing small effects of response to IFN and cellular stress, proteasome assembly and high activity of chaperone proteins. Similar differences are observed in pre_NACT samples, but without any MHC-II APP upregulation.

GO enrichment results have been clustered based on semantic similarity, and the best pathway (with the highest gene ratio) have been displayed. GSEA REACTOME results have been clustered based on the overrepresentation score and best pathway (highest absolute NES value) is being displayed

post_NACT: Macrophages

main upregulated genes:
CD9 - adhesion, motility, membrane fusion, signaling and protein trafficking, marker for exosomes, found to be important in OC (mixed outcomes)
IFI6 - IFN inducible protein, regulates apoptosis

HSP90AB1 - chaperone, stabilises new proteins and the ones partially denaturated (eg due to the cellular stress)

SOD2 - cleaning ROS in mitochondria, dealing with oxidative stress and acts antiapoptotic
BST2 - member of IFN-dependent antiviral response pathway, constitutes lipid rafts, limits diffusion of exosomes
SPINT2 - serine proteases inhibitor - presumably tumor suppressor, inhibits active HGF formation 
S100A11 - influences cytoskeleton dynamics and endo/exocytosis

SOD2 - cleaning ROS in mitochondria, dealing with oxidative stress and acts antiapoptotic
CLU - chaperon binds to misfolded proteins

stroma enriched

tumor enriched

mrna processing
translation
viral infection
homotypic cell adhesion
response to chemical stress, mitochondrial protein degradation, autophagy

ECM organisation
surface interactions on the vascular wall
regulation of myeloid cell differentiation

Summary of DCs stroma vs tumor PRE

pre-NACT tumor:
response to stress, autophagy mitochondrial proteins degradation
homotypic cell adhesion

pre-NACT stroma:
ECM organisation
regulation (pos/neg?) of IL-12, IL2, TNFa, TGFb production
regulation (pos/neg) of immune functions mediated by LAPTM5, PTPRC, TYROBP, CCR7, CST7, IL7R
collagen metabolism
complement activation
negative regulation of APP

hypothesis: 
main DCs activity happens in stroma, regulated in a complex manner (both pos and neg) by LAPTM5, PTPRC, TYROBP, CCR7, CST7, IL7R and causing regulation/production of L-12, IL2, TNFa, TGFb. Stromal DCs actively interacts with ECM, but in comparison to post-NACT samples, their APP MHC-II decreases. Tumor infiltrated DCs again are showing some effects of cellular stress and cell adhesion.

no activation of MHC-II APP in stroma as in post_NACT !
GeoMx results GSEA ct fraction correlation

Activity of key immune related pathways in Macrophages are correlated with fractions of other immune cell types in stromal compartments of post-NACT samples

spearman correlation between normalised ssGSEA pathway activity score and fraction of immune cells in given ROI

only correlation > 0.3 or <-0.3 are shown

SUMMARY: pathways activity corr with ct fraction (0.3-0.5)

Stroma post weak correlations:
positive corr with nr of Macrophages:
inflammatory response
M2 signature
APP
macrophage migration and differentiation
phagocytosis
IL-10
IL-4/13 
neutrophil degranulation
negative corr with nr of Macrophages:
(IL6-JAK-STAT in roiimmune)

negative corr with nr of Bcells:
inflammatory response
negative corr with nr of other immune cells (mostly Tregs and Th17):
MHCII APP
macrophage migration
apoptosis
IL2-JAK-STAT
TGFb
(CD80_CTLA4 + VEGF in roi immune)
CSF1
IL17
IL15

Macrophages dominated stromal regions of post-NACT samples

MACROPHAGES DOMINATED ROIs: enrichment in immune activation, chemotaxis, innate immune response and MHC-II antigen presentation

positive correlation between Macrophages fraction and inflammatory response, M2, APP, chemotaxis, activation, differentiation, IL-10, IL-4 and IL-13 signalling

Macrophages

ssGSEA signatures activity scores, DGE and GO enrichment calculated in cell-type specific deconvoluted transcriptomics profiles
pearson corr coeff > 0.3 and < -0.3 have been shown

correlation between ROI immune fraction and Macrophages ssGSEA scores

Macrophages

B-cells

other immune cells

negative correlation between B-cells fraction and inflammatory response, as well as other immune cells fraction and migration, APP via MHCII, apoptosis, IL2, and TGFb signatures

KEY RESULTS - immune cells residing in stroma vs infiltrating tumor mass

Immune cell distribution (to be confirmed in WSI):
Macrophages, CD8+ Tcells and B-cells are localized predominantly within stromal compartments
Macrophages residing in stromal compartment have increased activity of activation, immune response, inflammation, APP on MHCII, phagocytosis and cell adhesion signatures
Macrophages infiltrating tumor mass have increased activity of apoptosis, proliferation, stress response, L-10 production and glycolysis signatures
CD8+ T-cells residing in stromal compartment have increased activity of activation, differentiation, immune response, APP, cell adhesion and cytotoxicity signatures

KEY RESULTS - Immune cells colocalisation and it effect on macrophages gene expression in stromal compartments of post-NACT samples

Immune cells colocalization reveals 5 distinct clusters:
Macrophage dominated
Macrophage and CD8+ T-cells dominated
B-cells dominated
mixed with CD4+ T-cells
mixed with other T-cells (Tregs and Th17) 
Macrophages colocalized predominantly with other macrophages have higher activity of inflammatory response, M2, APP, migration, activation, differentiation and phagocytosis signatures. 
Macrophages activity is (weakly) negatively correlated with fraction of other cell types:
inflammatory response negatively correlates with nr of B-cells
migration, APP via MHCII, apoptosis, IL2, TGFb signatures correlates negatively with nr of other T-cells (Tregs and Th17)

Motivation of network analysis for immune ecosystems

Network analysis

Cells connected using distance threshold (here15 microns)

Connected components filtered with cell count threshold 10

Network of connected components

Previously collected connected components of the single cell networks are now connected using distance threshold (here 300 microns)

Each point represents group of > 10 cells

Community detection using Leiden algorithm

The network is partitioned into communities by maximizing a quality function called modularity

Algorithm aims to maximize connections within communities while minimizing connections between communities

Clustering of communities

Three types of simulations

CSR (complete spatial randomness):

All cell types generated independently

All cells placed randomly using poisson point process

Clustering amongst the cells of same type (based on Thomas process) :

Three cell type specific parameters:

Clustering probabilities
Cluster size ranges
Cluster spreads

Some cells become centers of clusters based on the clustering probabilities

There is possibility of nested clustering
(cluster inside cluster)

Interacting cell clusters:

Each cell type has specified affinity (probability score) towards other cells

When cell becomes a center of a cluster, the probabilities are used to determine the cell types of the cells in the cluster.

No visible interactions

clustered in tumor

Abstract EACR 2024, Cancer Genomics, Multiomics and Computational
Biology

Bergamo, Italy

**Integration of highly-multiplexed imaging and spatial transcriptomics
reveal contact-dependent differences in key immune-related molecular
pathways and cell states in ovarian cancer**

Iga Niemiec^1^, Ada Junquera^1^, Erdogan Pekcan Erkan^2^, Essi
Kahelin^1,3^, Matilda Salko^1^, Anna Vähärautio^1^, Ulla-Maija
Haltia^1,4^, Anni Virtanen^1,3^, Anniina Färkkilä^1,4,5,6^

1\) Research Program in Systems Oncology, Faculty of Medicine,
University of Helsinki

2\) Faculty of Medicine and Health Technology, Tampere University and
TAYS Cancer Centre, Tampere, Finland 

3\) Department of Pathology, University of Helsinki and HUS Diagnostic
Center, Helsinki

University Hospital, Helsinki, Finland

4\) Department of Obstetrics and Gynecology, and Clinical trials unit,
Comprehensive Cancer Centre, Helsinki University Hospital, Helsinki,
Finland

5\) Institute for Molecular Medicine Finland, Helsinki Institute of Life
Sciences, University of Helsinki, Finland

6\) iCAN Digital Precision Cancer Medicine

The tumor microenvironment (TME) profoundly influences the response to
neoadjuvant chemotherapy (NACT) in high-grade serous ovarian cancer
(HGSC). CD8 T-cells and macrophages are among the most prevalent cell
types within HGSC TME and understanding the interactions between them is

in high-grade serous ovarian cancer
(HGSC). CD8 T-cells and macrophages are among the most prevalent cell
types within HGSC TME and understanding the interactions between them is
essential for patient stratification and the development of novel
combination therapies. The advent of spatial transcriptomics
technologies enable comprehensive investigations of TME encompassing not
only quantitative, qualitative, but also spatial dimensions.

This study investigates cell states within high-grade serous ovarian
cancer (HGSC) samples\' regions of interest (ROIs), categorized by CD8
T-cell and macrophage colocalization (CD8+macrophage+, CD8-macrophage+,
CD8+macrophage-, CD8-macrophage-). Leveraging 11 post-NACT HGSC samples
and multiplex fluorescent imaging (tCyCIF), we selected 111 of such
ROIs. Subsequently, we identified the corresponding areas on adjacent
slides and using whole transcriptome spatial transcriptomics (GeoMX)
sequencing, we analyzed tumor and intratumoral stromal compartments\'
gene expression profiles. The dataset comprises 16,727 genes, with an
average of 7450 genes per compartment.

For each of the 222 compartments, we assessed cell type composition, as
well as immune-related signaling pathway activity, and cell states using
gene set variation analysis (GSVA) and gene ontology (GO) enrichment
analysis. Significant differences in certain immune-related pathways and

thway activity, and cell states using
gene set variation analysis (GSVA) and gene ontology (GO) enrichment
analysis. Significant differences in certain immune-related pathways and
cell states emerged between stromal and tumor compartments revealing
higher overall immune activity in peritumoral stroma. Among ROIs with
varied CD8/macrophage colocalization patterns, we've observed
upregulation of PD1, CTLA4 signaling, as well as a shift towards M2
macrophages being more prevalent within CD8+macrophage+ ROIs. Notably,
some changes, such as those within IFN-γ, IL2-STAT signaling, and MHC
presentation, correlated positively with patients\' progression-free
survival.

This study underscores the critical role of CD8+ T-cell--macrophage
interaction in NACT response, unveiling contact-dependent molecular
mechanisms that offer new therapeutic targets. By elucidating the
dynamics of the TME, our findings contribute to personalized treatment
strategies for HGSC patients.

Abstract EMBO Spatial Omics Workshop 2024,

22-25.10.2024, Rome, Italy

**Integration of highly-multiplexed imaging and spatial transcriptomics
reveal contact-dependent differences in cell-type composition and
cell-type specific molecular pathways in ovarian cancer**

Iga Niemiec^1^, Aleksandra Shabanova^1^, Ada Junquera^1^, Erdogan Pekcan
Erkan^2^, Essi Kahelin^1,3^, Matilda Salko^1^, Anna Vähärautio^1^,
Ulla-Maija Haltia^1,4^, Anni Virtanen^1,3^, Anniina Färkkilä^1,4,5,6^

1\) Research Program in Systems Oncology, Faculty of Medicine,
University of Helsinki

2\) Faculty of Medicine and Health Technology, Tampere University and
TAYS Cancer Centre, Tampere, Finland 

3\) Department of Pathology, University of Helsinki and HUS Diagnostic
Center, Helsinki

University Hospital, Helsinki, Finland

4\) Department of Obstetrics and Gynecology, and Clinical trials unit,
Comprehensive Cancer Centre, Helsinki University Hospital, Helsinki,
Finland

5\) Institute for Molecular Medicine Finland, Helsinki Institute of Life
Sciences, University of Helsinki, Finland

6\) iCAN Digital Precision Cancer Medicine

The tumor microenvironment (TME) profoundly influences the response to
neoadjuvant chemotherapy (NACT) in high-grade serous ovarian cancer
(HGSC). CD8 T-cells and macrophages are among the most prevalent cell
types within HGSC TME, but the growing evidence suggests that other

NACT) in high-grade serous ovarian cancer
(HGSC). CD8 T-cells and macrophages are among the most prevalent cell
types within HGSC TME, but the growing evidence suggests that other
immune cells such as dendritic cells (DCs), natural killer cells (NKs)
and B-cells may be as much important for shaping anti-tumor response.
Thus, studying the composition of TME and interaction between different
cell types is essential for patient stratification and the development
of novel combination therapies.

This study investigates cell-type composition and cell states within
HGSC samples\' regions of interest (ROIs), categorized by CD8 T-cell and
macrophage colocalization (CD8+macrophage+, CD8-macrophage+,
CD8+macrophage-, CD8-macrophage-). Leveraging 16 pre and post-NACT HGSC
samples and multiplex fluorescent imaging (tCyCIF), we selected 168 of
such ROIs. Subsequently, we identified the corresponding areas on
adjacent slides and using spatial transcriptomics (GeoMX) sequencing, we
analyzed tumor and intratumoral stromal compartments\' gene expression
profiles. The dataset comprises 16,727 genes, with an average of 7450
genes per compartment.

For each of 316 compartments, we deconvoluted spatial transcriptomics
signals to obtain the number of different cell types. We utilized two
different methods (SpatialDecon and BayesPrism), and compared the

ts, we deconvoluted spatial transcriptomics
signals to obtain the number of different cell types. We utilized two
different methods (SpatialDecon and BayesPrism), and compared the
results with the ground-truth information from cell-type calling on the
corresponding region of adjacent tCycIF slide,which enabled us to obtain
an accurate prediction of cell type fractions and cell-type specific
transcriptional profiles. We found that the fractions of DCs, B-cells
and NK cells correlate with the CD8+ T-cells--macrophages
co-localisation patterns and change in response to NACT treatment.
Additionally, we observed substantial differences in the activity of the
key immune-regulatory pathways (such as IFN-γ, IL2-STAT signaling, and
MHC presentation) in cell-type specific transcriptional profiles, in
response to macrophages and T-cells presence as well as NACT treatment.

Overall our data suggest that DCs, B-cells and NKs are active players
within TME and that their transcriptional profiles change with the
proximity to other cell types. This study underscores the critical role
of immune cells' interaction in NACT response, unveiling
contact-dependent transcriptional profiles that offer new potential
therapeutic targets.

Abstract SCSO 2025,

09-12.09.2025, Leuven, Belgium

**Integration of highly-multiplexed imaging and spatial transcriptomics
reveal contact-dependent differences in immune related transcriptional
pathways in ovarian cancer**

Iga Niemiec^1^, Gayani Anandagoda^1^, Aleksandra Shabanova^1^, Zhihan
Liang^1^, Ada Junquera^1^, Erdogan Pekcan Erkan^2^, Essi Kahelin^1,3^,
Matilda Salko^1^, Ulla-Maija Haltia^1,4^, Anni Virtanen^1,3^, Ladislav
Hovan^5^, Marieke Kuijjer^5,6,7^, Anna Vähärautio^1^, Anniina
Färkkilä^1,4,8^

1\) Research Program in Systems Oncology, Faculty of Medicine,
University of Helsinki

2\) Faculty of Medicine and Health Technology, Tampere University and
TAYS Cancer Centre, Tampere, Finland 

3\) Department of Pathology, University of Helsinki and HUS Diagnostic
Center, Helsinki

University Hospital, Helsinki, Finland

4\) Department of Obstetrics and Gynecology, and Clinical trials unit,
Comprehensive Cancer Centre, Helsinki University Hospital, Helsinki,
Finland

5\) The Norwegian Centre for Molecular Biosciences and Medicine (NCMBM),
Nordic EMBL Partnership, University of Oslo, Oslo 0318, Norway

6\) Department of Biochemistry and Developmental Biology, University of
Helsinki, Helsinki, Finland

icine (NCMBM),
Nordic EMBL Partnership, University of Oslo, Oslo 0318, Norway

6\) Department of Biochemistry and Developmental Biology, University of
Helsinki, Helsinki, Finland

7\) iCAN Flagship in Digital Precision Cancer Medicine, University of
Helsinki, Helsinki, Finland

8\) Institute for Molecular Medicine Finland, Helsinki Institute of Life
Sciences, University of Helsinki, Finland

Tumor microenvironment (TME) significantly affects neoadjuvant
chemotherapy (NACT) response in high-grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSC).
While CD8 T-cells and macrophages dominate within the HGSC TME, new
research underscores the role of CD4 T-cells and dendritic cells (DCs)
in shaping anti-tumor responses. Understanding TME composition and cell
interactions is vital for patient stratification and novel therapy
development.

We explored transcriptional cell states in HGSC, focusing on CD8 T-cell,
CD4 T-cell, DC, and macrophage colocalization patterns. Analyzing 26
post-NACT HGSC samples with multiplex fluorescent imaging (tCyCIF), we
identified 178 regions of interest (ROIs). Through spatial
transcriptomics (GeoMX) sequencing of corresponding regions on adjacent
slides, we analyzed gene expression in tumor and stromal compartments
separately, generating two areas of illumination (AOIs) per ROI. Our
dataset includes 18,122 genes, averaging 6,762 genes and a 0.37 gene
detection rate per AOI.

mal compartments
separately, generating two areas of illumination (AOIs) per ROI. Our
dataset includes 18,122 genes, averaging 6,762 genes and a 0.37 gene
detection rate per AOI.

We performed differential gene expression (DGE) analysis of AOIs with
different immune cell colocalization patterns, followed by gene set
enrichment analysis (GSEA) of 10 661 MsigDB signatures. We found that
key immune-regulatory pathways activation is connected with immune cell
colocalization. Notably, IFN-γ and IL2-STAT signaling increased in
stroma regions with CD8 T-cells and macrophages, while MHC-II pathways
were more active in tumor AOIs where CD4 or CD8 T-cells colocalize with
macrophages or DCs, suggesting enhanced antigen presentation within
cells infiltrating tumor mass. Further ligand-receptor analysis via
MultiNicheNetR on BayesPrism deconvoluted transcriptomics profiles, as
well as gene network reconstruction using PyPanda and Lioness tools,
identified critical interactions.

Our results highlight the importance of immune cell interactions in HGSC
TME, revealing contact-dependent transcriptional profiles that deepen
our understanding of the interaction dynamics of immune cells within
TME.

**\###################################################**

**\# EACR 2024**

**Analysis of spatial colocalization of CD8 T-cells and macrophages
within high-grade serous ovarian cancer samples reveal contact-dependent
differences in key immune-related molecular pathways and cell states.**

Iga Niemiec^1^, Ada Junquera^1^, Erdogan Pekcan Erkan^2^, Anna
Vähärautio^1^, Anniina Färkkilä^1,3,4,5^

1\) Research Program in Systems Oncology, University of Helsinki,
Finland

2\) Faculty of Medicine and Health Technology, Tampere University and
TAYS Cancer Centre, Tampere, Finland 

3\) Institute for Molecular Medicine Finland, Helsinki Institute of Life
Sciences, University of Helsinki, Finland

4\) Department of Obstetrics and Gynecology, Helsinki University
Hospital, Helsinki, Finland

5\) iCAN Digital Precision Cancer Medicine, Finland

CD8 T-cells and macrophages play crucial roles in high-grade serous
ovarian cancer (HGSC) tumor microenvironment (TME), and many studies
have revealed their predictive role in determining patient response to
neoadjuvant chemotherapy (NACT). Understanding the interactions between
them is essential for patient stratification and the development of
novel combination therapies. With the advent of spatial transcriptomics
technologies, studying this interaction in terms of not only
quantitative and qualitative, but also spatial aspects have become
possible.

ith the advent of spatial transcriptomics
technologies, studying this interaction in terms of not only
quantitative and qualitative, but also spatial aspects have become
possible.

This study aims to investigate the cell states within regions of
interests (ROIs) within HGSC samples, which differs in the
colocalization of CD8 T-cells and macrophages (CD8+macrophage+,
CD8-macrophage+, CD8+macrophage-, CD8-macrophage-). To reach that goal,
we utilized 11 HGSC post-NACT samples and using multiplex fluorescent
imaging technology (tCyCIF), we identified \~10 ROI per sample, based on
the presence or absence of CD8 T-cells and macrophages. Next, we
identified the same areas on the adjacent slides and performed spatial
transcriptomics (GeoMX) sequencing, separately for tumor and
intratumoral stromal compartment.

Next, using deconvolution methods, we estimated the number of cells in
each compartment, and estimated the activity of various immune-related
signaling pathways and cell states using gene set variation analysis
(GSVA) and gene ontology (GO) enrichment analysis. Our study revealed
differences in the activity of many key immune-related pathways and cell
states both between immune cells residing in the stromal vs tumor
compartments, as well as ROIs with different CD8+/macrophages
collocalisation patterns. Moreover, some of the identified changes have

immune cells residing in the stromal vs tumor
compartments, as well as ROIs with different CD8+/macrophages
collocalisation patterns. Moreover, some of the identified changes have
been positively correlated with the patients progression free survival
(PFS). These results support the idea of the vital role of CD8+ T-cells
-- macrophage interaction in patients\' response to NACT therapy and
reveal the possible underlying molecular mechanisms, which may serve as
potential targets for future therapies.

**\##################################################**

**\# AACR 2023**

**Spatial patterns of immune cell states are associated with poor
response to neoadjuvant chemotherapy in high-grade serous ovarian
cancer.**

The composition and functional state of the tumor microenvironment (TME)
play a crucial role in determining the response to neoadjuvant
chemotherapy (NACT) in high-grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSC) patients.
Understanding these complex interactions is essential for patient
stratification and the development of novel combination therapies. With
the advent of spatial transcriptomics technologies, studying the TME in
terms of not only quantitative, qualitative, but also spatial aspects
have become possible.

This study aimed to investigate the spatial distribution and functional
states of different cell types within the HGSC TME and their association
with NACT response. We utilized 10 publicly available post-NACT HGSC 10x
Visium spatial transcriptomics datasets comprising patients with good
(n=3), partial (n=2), and poor (n=5) responses to NACT. Each spot on
Visium hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) slides has been manually annotated by
a pathologist as tumor, mixed (both tumor and normal morphology), stroma
or other. Three tumor-stromal interface (TSI) zones were defined based
on proximity to tumor/mixed spots (TSI1 closest to the tumor, followed
by TSI2 and TSI3).

rphology), stroma
or other. Three tumor-stromal interface (TSI) zones were defined based
on proximity to tumor/mixed spots (TSI1 closest to the tumor, followed
by TSI2 and TSI3).

Differential gene expression (DGE) analysis followed by
overrepresentation analysis (ORA) was performed to characterize
transcriptional pathway activity variations between tumor, TSI, and
stromal spots. Notably, differential activity was observed in crucial
pathways such as epithelial-mesenchymal transition, hypoxia,
transforming growth factor beta (TGFb), and tumor necrosis factor alpha
(TNFa) signaling, both within spot types and across different NACT
responses.

Additionally, analysis of cell type distribution within the zones was
conducted. UCell scores for HGSC TME cell type signatures were
calculated for each spot, and cell type deconvolution was performed
using the FindTransferAnchor method from the Seurat R package.
Comparisons of mean UCell and deconvolution scores across spot types
revealed substantial spatial variations in cell type composition,
particularly for macrophages, fibroblasts, and plasma cells. Notably,
these spatial distributions showed significant changes with the NACT
response. For instance, macrophage scores were markedly higher in good
responders and decreased with distance from the tumor. Fibroblast
signatures were significantly elevated in good responders but only in

rophage scores were markedly higher in good
responders and decreased with distance from the tumor. Fibroblast
signatures were significantly elevated in good responders but only in
close proximity to the tumor, while plasma cell scores were notably
elevated exclusively within tumor and mixed spots of good responders.
Furthermore, selected functional properties of these cell types were
examined by computing UCell scores for crucial pathways governing their
biology.

Collectively, our findings highlight substantial differences in TME
composition depending on proximity to the tumor and NACT response.
Macrophages, fibroblasts, and plasma cells exhibited the most prominent
spatial patterns. These results underscore the importance of studying
the TME within a spatial context to gain deeper insights into these
intricate interactions, so important to the NACT response.

### **Spatial multi-omic integration reveals molecular programs of chemotherapy-remodeled immune communities in ovarian cancer (120/120 characters)**

**Authors**: Aleksandra Shabanova 1, Iga Niemiec 1, Zhihan Liang 1,
Saundarya Shah 1, Ada Junquera

1, Elias Ruuska 1, Ziqi Kang 1, [Maija Tissarinen 1,]{.mark} Gayani
Anandagoda 1, Anni Virtanen 1,3, Matilda Salko 1, Ulla-Maija Haltia 1,2,
Anniina Färkkilä 1,2,3,4

1 Research Program in Systems Oncology, University of Helsinki,
Helsinki, Finland

2 Department of Obstetrics and Gynecology, Department of Oncology,
Clinical Trials Unit, Comprehensive

Cancer Center, Helsinki University Hospital, Helsinki, Finland

3 Department of Pathology, University of Helsinki and HUS Diagnostic
Center, Helsinki University

Hospital, Helsinki, Finland.

4 Institute for Molecular Medicine Finland, Helsinki Institute for Life
Sciences, University of Helsinki, Helsinki, Finland

**Introduction**

Chemotherapy significantly changes the tumor immune microenvironment
(TIME) in high-grade serous carcinoma (HGSC). While immune aggregates
can predict survival in treatment-naïve patients, their predictive value
often diminishes after chemotherapy due to diverse, patient-specific
mechanisms of immune evasion. We hypothesize that immune evasion is a
complex process shaped by multicellular networks that determine
functional states and drive resistance.

ecific
mechanisms of immune evasion. We hypothesize that immune evasion is a
complex process shaped by multicellular networks that determine
functional states and drive resistance. By studying molecular mechanisms
driving these patterns, this project aims to uncover novel prognostic
markers and therapeutic targets, advancing personalized precision
immunotherapies for HGSC patients.

**Cohort & methods**

We combined spatial proteomics (t-CycIF) and spatial transcriptomics
(GeoMx) to analyze 58 HGSC samples from the Oncosys-Ova study, with 14
paired pre- and post-chemotherapy tissues. A semi-automated pipeline
facilitated GeoMx ROI selection by identifying overlapping immune marker
signals in adjacent t-CycIF images. This workflow enabled targeted
transcriptomic profiling of immune communities, with all samples linked
to comprehensive clinical and sequencing data.

**Results**

In total, we quantified 6 distinct cell populations across the tumor
microenvironment: macrophages, cancer, dendritic, CD8+ CD4+ T, and
stromal cells, along with 1,180 whole-transcriptome segments. Using our
in-house framework, SPACEStat, we modeled t-CycIF data as spatial
cellular networks and quantified immune communities for all present
immune cell combinations. Our framework also allowed us to integrate
protein-level spatial architecture and molecular programs. We achieved

fied immune communities for all present
immune cell combinations. Our framework also allowed us to integrate
protein-level spatial architecture and molecular programs. We achieved
high cross-platform correlation (R\>0.65) in cell-type fractions,
successfully mapping over 80% of transcriptomic segments to
SPACEstat-detected immune communities from the TIME network.

Our integrated atlas reveals that post-treatment HGSC immune communities
are highly patient-specific and are reshaped by chemotherapy relative to
pre-chemo samples, with significantly greater spatial diversity in the
post-treatment environment. By integrating these spatial
immunophenotypes with clinico-molecular data via statistical modeling,
we identified recurrent immunosuppressive tissue motifs linked to
specific functional states. Ultimately, we demonstrate that spatial
organization, rather than simple cell density, is the primary
characteristic of the post-chemotherapy immune landscape in HGSC.

**Conclusion**

In conclusion, this study delivers precise spatial immunophenotyping,
illuminating the context of immune cell interactions and their molecular
programs. Our findings identify actionable targets and establish a
foundation for precision immunotherapies in the post-chemotherapy
landscape of HGSC.

Tumor progression relies on immune evasion, and many high-grade serous
carcinoma (HGSC) patients show limited response to immunotherapies. Our
goal is to combine spatial multi-omic profiling with machine learning to
identify immune evasion strategies and discover biomarkers and
therapeutic targets for precision oncology.

This project analyzed samples from the Oncosys-Ova study, including 41
FFPE tumor tissues---14 of which were chemo-naïve & treated
pairs---alongside clinical and genomic data. We used t-CycIF imaging to

amples from the Oncosys-Ova study, including 41
FFPE tumor tissues---14 of which were chemo-naïve & treated
pairs---alongside clinical and genomic data. We used t-CycIF imaging to
assess the spatial protein expression of ten markers across tumor,
stromal, and immune compartments. Adjacent FFPE slides underwent
Nanostring GeoMx spatial transcriptomics using t-CycIF-guided regions of
interest.

We developed a semi-automated method for ROI selection based on immune
markers (CD4, CD8, CD11c, Iba1, NKG2a). After denoising raw images and
enhancing signal clusters, we identified overlapping marker signals and
selected ROIs using size filtering and manual assessment in Napari. We
collected 10 ROIs from each of 13 samples and a median of 17 ROIs from
an additional 28 samples, ensuring diverse immune hub representation.
Spatial transcriptomics was then performed on matched ROIs to evaluate
gene expression in tumor and stromal compartments---210 QC compartments
from 13 samples and \~800 from the remaining ones.

Combining these data layers with clinical and genomic information
created a high-resolution map of the spatial molecular landscape in
HGSC. Using our SPACEstat toolkit for spatial point pattern and network
analysis, we identified highly patient-specific immune hubs shaped by
diverse microenvironments. These findings highlight the heterogeneity of

or spatial point pattern and network
analysis, we identified highly patient-specific immune hubs shaped by
diverse microenvironments. These findings highlight the heterogeneity of
the tumor immune microenvironment and the need for integrated,
multi-modal analyses.

Our ongoing work applies machine learning to consolidate spatial and
molecular features, aiming to uncover immune vulnerabilities and inform
the design of targeted immunotherapies for HGSC.

Speaker notes:
high-grade serous cancer (HGSC), is the most common and aggresive subtype of ovarian cancer.
The treatment approaches for the initial management of newly diagnosed ovarian cancer encompass two main paradigms. The first option is primary debulking surgery (PDS) followed by combination platinum-based chemotherapy. On the other hand, when PDS could be challenging to perform, neoadjuvant chemotherapy (NACT) is administered first. Next, it is followed by interval debulking surgery (IDS) and further chemotherapy after surgery.
However, currently, patients has 50% have 5 year mortality rate.

https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30813239/

Speaker notes:
Around 50% of epithelial ovarian cancer patients exhibit intraepithelial tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes (TILs), which correlate with prolonged survival and suggest a presence of immunoreactive subset. Because immune checkpoint blockade (e.g., PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors) depends on preexisting TILs, some patients should respond; however, clinical trials have shown limited efficacy, likely due to the absence of reliable biomarkers to identify responders.

A single biomarker is insufficient to predict benefit from immune checkpoint inhibitors in ovarian cancer, as whole tumor microenvironment (TME) was shown to play a critical role in regulating cancer immunity.

Therefore, a comprehensive assessment of TME—including immune cells’ distribution, subsets, functionality, and their interactions with other cells—is essential for improving patient selection and therapeutic outcomes.

Speaker notes:
As a next step, we will explore the immune microenvironment by analyzing immune networks and their spatial interactions.
Then, we will integrate single-cell t-CyCIF and mini-bulk GeoMx data by representing the immune landscape as a network of interacting nodes, where selected regions of interest (ROIs) are covered by GeoMx profiling.
This network framework will then be enriched with available clinico-molecular patient data to uncover mechanisms of immune regulation within the tumor microenvironment—ultimately aiming to identify novel biomarkers and therapeutic targets.

It preprocesses the selected channels to identify the immune hubs. Here, as examples, we have CD4 and CD8 channels, where the final mask image shows poteantial immune hubs of corresponding cell type.
The tool identifies the overlapping hubs between selected channels and proposes candidate ROIs based on the size
Finally, human is required to inspect candidate ROIs and select final tentative ROIs

In total, we are using following intregrated spatial protein and transcriptomic profilimg for 5 immune cells. Studing their interactions in stroma and tumor.

We performed preliminary analysis of 14 tCycIF samples. The images were segmneted and cell phenotyyped to receive final singke cell table with their spatial coordinates and phenotype. This dataset is then used in SPACEStat package to study spatial distribution on singkle-cell level. Firstly, by qunatifying global distances, as median distance between two cell pairs with relfected stength of their spatial closeness calculated as AUC of Index of nearest neighbour measurement collected vs distance measured, we showed strong proximity of cells of same type, and next T cell and Myeloids. Next, we turned to the tumor–stroma interface — the critical frontline where tumors and immune cells meet.

wed strong proximity of cells of same type, and next T cell and Myeloids. Next, we turned to the tumor–stroma interface — the critical frontline where tumors and immune cells meet. We identified tumor-stromal interface cells as a cell has at least one neighbor from the other group within 35 um.

Then, we calculated both average distance of each cell type to the TSI, as well as the tumor cluster density with DBSCAN (lower density mean …). Between sample variability is evidently striking here. Across samples, the tumor–stroma interface distance can range widely — from as little as 50 microns to as much as 150. Yet within a single sample, distances remain relatively stable, with the largest gap between two immune populations reaching about 80 to 160 microns.
What’s surprising is that these distances don’t align with tumor cluster density. Even samples with very long interface distances can show tumor densities similar to those with much shorter distances. This suggests that factors beyond simple tumor density are shaping immune positioning at the tumor–stroma boundary.

Finally, We defined enrichment as the fold change between the observed number of interactions and the average number from ten rounds of random shuffling. A fold change greater than one means enrichment, equal to one means no change, and less than one indicates depletion.
We performed this analysis for two, three and four immune cell types with increasing radii, from 30 microns to 120 with step of 45 microns.

Such analysis revealed myeloid and t cell attraction in half of samples, compared to second half, but interestingly it didn’t capture triplets and quadroplets -> fold change of ~0.5

These results highlight how diverse and complex the tumor immune microenvironment is across patients, and they underscore the importance of deeper, multi-modal analysis of both spatial features (on whole sample level) and patient data.

Spatial patterns of immune cell states are associated with poor response to neoadjuvant chemotherapy in high-grade serous ovarian cancer

Iga Niemiec1, Essi Kahelin1,2, Zhihan Liang1, Erdogan Pekcan Erkan3, Anna Vähärautio1, Anni Virtanen1,2, Anniina Färkkilä1,4,5,6

INTRODUCTION

ACKNOWLEDGEMENTS

The composition and functional state of the tumor microenvironment (TME) play a crucial role in determining response to neoadjuvant chemotherapy (NACT) in high-grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSC) patients. Understanding these complex interactions is essential for patient stratification and development of novel combination therapies. This study aimed to investigate the spatial distribution and functional states of different cell types within TME and their association with NACT response, in Visium spatial transcriptomics HGSC samples.

Our findings highlight substantial differences in TME composition depending on proximity to the tumor and NACT response. Macrophages, fibroblasts, T cells and plasma cells exhibited the most prominent spatial patterns. These results underscore the importance of studying the TME within a spatial context to gain deeper insights into these intricate interactions, so important to the NACT response.

INTRODUCTION

ACKNOWLEDGEMENTS & CONTACT

Tumor microenvironment (TME) profoundly influences the response to neoadjuvant chemotherapy (NACT) in high-grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSC). CD8 T-cells and macrophages are considered the key players within HGSC TME, but other immune cells such as dendritic cells (DCs), natural killer cells (NKs) and B-cells may be as much important for shaping anti-tumor response. Understanding how the interactions between them changes through the course of NACT treatment is essential for development of novel combination therapies. 

Objectives of the study:
identification of immune cell types colocalizing with macrophages and CD8 T-cells
analysing correlations between immune cell abundance and activity of key processes of macrophage and T-cell immunity
examining how NACT treatment shapes the immune interactions within TME

Fractions of most cell types are higher within stroma segments
Fractions of DCs, Bcells and Mast cells are higher after NACT in both segments as is macrophage infiltration into tumor segment 

Regardless of segment and NACT treatment status:
Mast cells and NKs fractions are highly correlated and correlates negatively with the activity of macrophages MHC-I/II antigen presentation pathway and other immune functions of macrophages

and NKs fractions are highly correlated and correlates negatively with the activity of macrophages MHC-I/II antigen presentation pathway and other immune functions of macrophages
Macrophages fraction correlate positively with both T-cells fraction and their immune functions (most prominently in pre-NACT tumor segments)

Cell-type interactions affected by NACT treatment:
Bcells, Mast cells, DCs and NKs fractions correlate positively with macrophage activation only within pre-NACT tumor segments
NKs and Mast cells correlates negatively with T-cell functions only within pre-NACT stroma segments
DCs and T-cells fractions are highly correlated. DCs correlates positively with all T-cells immune functions only after NACT treatment, in both stroma and tumor segments

NACT treatment profoundly changes both immune cell-type abundance and interactions between them, differently for cells infiltrating tumor mass and localized within intratumoral stroma. Studying its effect for TME as a whole is necessary to find new molecular targets for HGSC combination therapies.

INTRODUCTION

ACKNOWLEDGEMENTS & CONTACT

Tumor microenvironment (TME) profoundly influences the response to neoadjuvant chemotherapy and overall survival in high-grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSC). CD8 T-cells and macrophages are considered the key players within HGSC TME, but other immune cells such as dendritic cells (DCs) and B-cells may be as much important for shaping anti-tumor response. Understanding how the interactions between them are affecting the TME is essential for development of novel combination therapies. 

Objectives of the study:
identification of immune cell types influencing key processes within HGSC intratumoral stroma
analysing how immune activity changes with the proximity to other immune cells and identifying cell-type specific contact-dependent differences in activity of key transcriptional pathways

Multiple key cancer signatures are correlated with immune cell types abundance in intratumoral stroma. As expected, these are immune signatures, but also some of the proliferation and signalling signatures.
Macrophages cell type fraction is highly correlated with elevated activity of key immune activation (MHC-I, MHC-II, complement) and pro-inflammatory signatures (IL-6-JAK-STAT3, inflammatory response, IFNg, TNFa), as well as the ones promoting tumor growth and proliferation (p53, IL2-STAT5 and androgen response).

ro-inflammatory signatures (IL-6-JAK-STAT3, inflammatory response, IFNg, TNFa), as well as the ones promoting tumor growth and proliferation (p53, IL2-STAT5 and androgen response). It highlights the known double-role of macrophages in TME where, on the one hand - they elevate anti-tumor immune response and pro-inflammatory environment (M2-like), but on the other - promotes tumor growth and cause immunesuppression (M1-like).
B-cells fraction is correlated with tumor-growth promoting signatures - DNA repair and MYC targets, and negatively correlated with immune response signature, which may suggest their immunosupressive role within TME. Although mild negative correlation with TNFa is also visible.
Proximity to other cell types can change the activity of the identified pathways within cell-type specific deconvoluted transcriptional signal. 
Macrophages pro-inflammatory signatures are significantly elevated in the presence of both CD8 T-cells (IFNg) and DCs (IFNg, IL6-JAK-STAT3), while the ones promoting tumor growth (p53, androgen response, IL2-STAT5, TNFa) are higher when these cell types are less abundant. That suggest that both CD8 T-cells and DCs may promote pro-inflammatory anti-cancer response of macrophages.

, IL2-STAT5, TNFa) are higher when these cell types are less abundant. That suggest that both CD8 T-cells and DCs may promote pro-inflammatory anti-cancer response of macrophages.
B-cells MYC targets signature is elevated with the higher proportion of DCs and lower proportion of macrophages in the neighborhood, suggesting the opposed role of this cell types on pro-cancer activity of B-cells.

Speaker notes:
We are working in our lab with high-grade serous cancer (HGSC), is the most common and aggresive subtype of ovarian cancer.
The treatment approaches for the initial management of newly diagnosed ovarian cancer encompass two main paradigms. The first option is primary debulking surgery (PDS) followed by combination platinum-based chemotherapy. On the other hand, when PDS could be challenging to perform, neoadjuvant chemotherapy (NACT) is administered first. Next, it is followed by interval debulking surgery (IDS) and further chemotherapy after surgery.
However, currently, patients has 50% have 5 year mortality rate.

Tumor microenviroment (TME) impact on cancer immunity & response to immunotherapies

Subset of patients have immunereactive subtype with tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes & ativated signaling pathways associated with immune cells

JAVELIN Solid Tumour | Avelumab | 9.6

KEYNOTE-028 | Pembrolizumab | 11.5

Speaker notes:
Around 50% of epithelial ovarian cancer patients exhibit intraepithelial tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes (TILs), which correlate with prolonged survival and suggest a presence of immunoreactive subset. Because immune checkpoint blockade (e.g., PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors) depends on preexisting TILs, some patients should respond; however, clinical trials have shown limited efficacy, likely due to the absence of reliable biomarkers to identify responders.

A single biomarker is insufficient to predict benefit from immune checkpoint inhibitors in ovarian cancer, as whole tumor microenvironment (TME) was shown to play a critical role in regulating cancer immunity.

cient to predict benefit from immune checkpoint inhibitors in ovarian cancer, as whole tumor microenvironment (TME) was shown to play a critical role in regulating cancer immunity. Therefore, a comprehensive assessment of TME—including immune cells’ distribution, subsets, functionality, and their interactions with other cells—is essential for improving patient selection and therapeutic outcomes.

Speaker notes:
spatial profiling methodologies can systematically capture and illuminate the physical localization of the diverse components within the TME
One notable approach is t-CyCIF, a cyclic microscopy method allowing multiplexed imaging of proteins within the TME.
It employs an iterative process of tissue staining with tagged antibodies, followed by imaging and signal inactivation, resulting in final images with multiple channels – protein targets.

Preliminary analysis of 14 samples – tCycIF image processing

https://github.com/farkkilab/image_processing/tree/main

Speaker notes:
Today I will share some of the preliminary analysis of 14 tCycIF samples. 
The raw data is preprocessed as follows:
Image correction & stitching – Tiles corrected for illumination and stitched into TIFF images using Ashlar.
Cell segmentation – Nuclei segmented with StarDist.
Feature extraction – Segmentation masks used to generate a table of cell coordinates, morphology, and marker/channel intensities.
Quality control – Quantified tables QC’d with Cylinter to remove false segmentations and artifact regions.
Cell phenotyping – Tribus allows semi-automated discovery of cell phenotypes based highly granular unsupervised clustering, followed by automated cluster annotation based on a prior-knowledge cell phenotyping scheme. For challenging markers, like NKs in our case, scimap is used for manual phenotyping
Visualization – Images can be inspected throughout the workflow using Napari.

Preliminary explorartion of TME by SPACEStat

A

C

B

Radius 50 µm

Speaker notes:
After preprocessing, we apply the SPACEStat package to perform spatial analysis:
Cell–cell distance distribution – median distance + enrichment (AUC).
Immune cell residency – neighbor proportions within 50 µm.
Immune infiltration index – derived from tumor and stroma density.
Tumor–stroma interface (TSI) cells – identification and characterization both global and per-slide.
Immune network analysis – uncovering complex immune interactions.

Today I will show some of the preliminary analsysis we received with this package

Preliminary exploration of TME by SPACEStat revealed spatial interaction and exclusion patterns across samples

highly sample-dependent

Speaker notes:
By looking at cell–cell distances across all 14 samples, we calculated the median distance between different cell types and assessed enrichment using the area under the curve. This analysis showed that T cells—both CD4 and CD8—tend to cluster more closely with myeloid cells, including macrophages and dendritic cells.
Next, we zoomed in on dendritic cells and CD4 T cells. Using the immune infiltration index—defined as the ratio of immune density in tumor versus stroma—together with tumor cluster density, we created a sample-specific scatterplot. What we saw was that, in most samples, dendritic cells and CD4 T cells are excluded from low-density tumor clusters.
Overall, these findings highlight both spatial interactions and exclusion patterns that shape the tumor microenvironment.

SPACEstat TSI identification (C) showed global and sample-specific immune cell–TSI proximity

highly sample-dependent

Speaker notes:
Lastly, we turned to the tumor–stroma interface — the critical frontline where tumors and immune cells meet. Using SPACEStat, we measured global distances for five major immune cell types across all samples. Interestingly, their distributions aligned closely, suggesting a shared pattern of immune positioning at this boundary.

SPACEstat TSI identification (C) showed global and sample-specific immune cell–TSI proximity

Example

Speaker notes:
When we shift from global averages to individual samples, the variability becomes striking. Across samples, the tumor–stroma interface distance can range widely — from as little as 50 microns to as much as 150. Yet within a single sample, distances remain relatively stable, with the largest gap between two immune populations reaching about 80 to 160 microns.
What’s surprising is that these distances don’t align with tumor cluster density. Even samples with very long interface distances can show tumor densities similar to those with much shorter distances. This suggests that factors beyond simple tumor density are shaping immune positioning at the tumor–stroma boundary.

Diverse Immune interaction hubs revealed by tCycIF & GeoMx

Hub enrichment is highly sample-dependent

Speaker notes:
Moving closer to the interaction hubs, we looked at enriched immune cell pairs. We defined enrichment as the fold change between the observed number of interactions and the average number from five rounds of random shuffling. A fold change greater than one means enrichment, equal to one means no change, and less than one indicates depletion.
When we plot these immune pairs across samples, what stands out is how sample-dependent the patterns are — a result that mirrors what we also see with GeoMx interaction hubs. Together, these findings highlight just how diverse and complex the tumor immune microenvironment is across patients, and they underscore the importance of deeper, multi-modal analysis of both spatial features and patient data.

Speaker notes:
As a next step, we will explore the immune microenvironment by analyzing immune networks and their spatial interactions.
Then, we will integrate single-cell t-CyCIF and mini-bulk GeoMx data by representing the immune landscape as a network of interacting nodes, where selected regions of interest (ROIs) are covered by GeoMx profiling.
This network framework will then be enriched with available clinico-molecular patient data to uncover mechanisms of immune regulation within the tumor microenvironment—ultimately aiming to identify novel biomarkers and therapeutic targets.

Research questions - transcriptomics based

Does transcriptional programmes of immune cells differ between stroma and tumor localisation?
How immune cells are changing upon infiltration? Are they becoming more stimulated or suppressed? Can we observe switching on specific IEMs? Is it correlated with patient OS/PFS/other clinical characteristics? 
Does transcriptional programmes of immune cells differ in the presence/absence of other immune cells? 
Which cells (and which pathways/LRs) stimulate/supress immune response of other immune cells? Can we observe switching on specific IEMs? Is it correlated with patient OS/PFS/other clinical characteristics? 
Does transcriptional programmes of immune cells differ depends on high-level spatial organisation (derived from tCycIF)?
 
How NACT treatment affects immune cells transcriptional profiles? Patient pairwise analysis.
Are there any consistent NACT-induced differences in immune cell transcriptomics profiles (tumor/stroma separately)? Is it correlated with patient OS/PFS/other clinical characteristics?

Tribus + manual gating

Newest approach:
Tribus phenotyping (Tumor/Stroma/CD8/CD4/Macrophages/DCs)
Manual gating (all gates)
Phenotypes refining:
If immune cells was found within Stroma/Tumor in tribus, but is negative for its cell marker (in manual gating) in manual gating → re-labled into Stroma/Tumor 
If cell was labelled as Tumor, but is positive in CD8 in manual gating → CD8
If cell is positive in manual gating for any of the additional markers (CD31, HEV, CD20) → CD31_Endothelial, HEV_Endothelial, B_cell respectively

vector_chunks.source_file: 4. Meetings and updates/EyeMT_ updates master presentation_.pptx
vector_chunks.text:
BANKSY - Spatial PCA

BANKSY is a method for clustering spatial omics data by augmenting the features of each cell with both an average of the features of its spatial neighbors along with neighborhood feature gradients. By incorporating neighborhood information for clustering, BANKSY is able to
improve cell-type assignment in noisy data
distinguish subtly different cell-types stratified by microenvironment
identify spatial domains sharing the same microenvironment
BANKSY is applicable to a wide array of spatial technologies (e.g. 10x Visium, Slide-seq, MERFISH, CosMX, CODEX) and scales well to large datasets.

vector_chunks.source_file: 4. Meetings and updates/EyeMT_ updates master presentation_.pptx

Example sample - 7-S026_iOme2

Step 1: Compute principal components (PCs) accounting for 20 spatial neighbors 
! Limited number of markers shared across batches - PanCK, Vimentin, Iba1, CD11c, CD4, CD8a + DNA?
It leads to minimal number of PCs

vector_chunks.source_file: 4. Meetings and updates/EyeMT_ updates master presentation_.pptx
vector_chunks.text:
Step 2: Clustering of cells based on PCs

! BANKCY built-in clustering is not working on so few PCs 
One of two approaches outside of BANKCY package - K-means with 6 centers

vector_chunks.source_file: 4. Meetings and updates/EyeMT_ updates master presentation_.pptx
vector_chunks.text:
Step 2: Clustering of cells based on PCs

One of two approaches outside of BANKCY package - Leiden clustering based on modularity – compares graph to a random version of itself, only grouping cells if they are significantly more connected than they would be by chance.

tCycIF + SPACEstat downstream analysis: next steps

Validation of our approach:
Simulation
Healthy omentum tissue - controlled heterogeneity asked by Tuomas 
To perform pair correlation test, we need to test what is the controlled structure to ensure that the distance we pick up to build the networks catches the true spatial organisation

vector_chunks.source_file: 4. Meetings and updates/EyeMT_ updates master presentation_.pptx
vector_chunks.text:
Three types of simulations

CSR (complete spatial randomness):

All cell types generated independently

All cells placed randomly using poisson point process

Clustering amongst the cells of same type (based on Thomas process) :

Three cell type specific parameters:

Clustering probabilities
Cluster size ranges
Cluster spreads

Some cells become centers of clusters based on the clustering probabilities

There is possibility of nested clustering
(cluster inside cluster)

Interacting cell clusters:

Each cell type has specified affinity (probability score) towards other cells

When cell becomes a center of a cluster, the probabilities are used to determine the cell types of the cells in the cluster.

vector_chunks.source_file: 4. Meetings and updates/EyeMT_ updates master presentation_.pptx
vector_chunks.text:
Pair correlation function

spatial statistic that measures the relative density of "neighbor" cells at a specific distance (r) from a "reference" cell

Question on full data (all cells): "If I am at a CD8+ T-cell, how much more (or less) likely am I to find another cell at distance r compared to a random distribution?"

Question on reduced data (network representation): "If I am at a CD8+ T-network component, how much more (or less) likely am I to find another network component at distance r compared to a random distribution?"

vector_chunks.source_file: 4. Meetings and updates/EyeMT_ updates master presentation_.pptx
vector_chunks.text:
The issue - adjustment of pair correlation value with controlled heterogeneity case

Standard analysis assumes tissue is a blank, uniform canvas.
But there is a natural structure of tissue - natural spread of immune cells in benign/borderline omentum, for instance.

Solution - inhomogeneous pair correlation function -> weighting factor

H&E segmented benign tumor - S457_iOme

Soon tCycIF

vector_chunks.source_file: 4. Meetings and updates/EyeMT_ updates master presentation_.pptx
vector_chunks.text:
Maija project ideas - data integration

Phase 1: primary approaches (would be great to assigned them as a focus of student)
1. Fully Integrated GAMM (that Iga proposed): Fitting a single hierarchical model where ROI-level data is the dependent variable.
Challenge: High risk of non-convergence or overfitting (capturing ROI noise instead of patient biology). A student can spend the time necessary to tune the smoothing parameters and random effects.
2. Two-step spatial phenotyping (Factor Analysis + GAMM): Reducing spatial metrics into few interpretable ‘spatial immunophenotypes’ before modeling.
Challenge: Risks information loss if two metrics with opposite biological effects are collapsed. The student can validate these factors against known biology to ensure we aren’t losing the signal.

Phase 2: Contingency models (If Phase 1 lacks power)
If the above approaches don’t yield stable results, I was considering following models as back-up:
Bayesian hierarchical modeling (via brms): Excellent for handling uncertainty in small datasets, though computationally expensive and requires careful prior specification.
Geographically weighted regression (GWR): Good for seeing how relationships shift across a slide, though it can lead to ‘fragmented’ results that are difficult to summarize at the patient level.

Phase 0:

Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA):

README.md

2026-05-26

Project 9 EyeMT
Project Overview

Integrating multiomics data to understand the role of diverse immune cell types—specifically T-cells and
myeloid cells—and their spatial interactions in shaping cancer immune escape mechanisms and overall
patient clinical outcomes.

Core Data Modalities

Proteomics: Cyclic Immunofluorescence (tCycIF)
Spatial Transcriptomics: GeoMx (performed on adjacent tissue slides)
Genomics: Whole Exome Sequencing (WES) via iCAN database

Responsible Personnel

Iga Niemiec

CycIF Quality Control & Spatial Analysis
Data Exploration & Statistical Integration

Integrating multiomics data to understand the role of diverse immune cell types—specifically T-cells and
myeloid cells—and their spatial interactions in shaping cancer immune escape mechanisms and overall
patient clinical outcomes.

Building a computational pipeline for ligand receptor interaction analysis in GeoMx spatial transcrip-
tomics data to investigate tumor-immune microenvironment interactions in ovarian cancer.
Master’s thesis July 1, 2025 48
HGSC, tumor microenvironment, ligand receptor analysis, spatial transcriptomics, BioinformaticsHigh-grade serous ovarian carcinoma (HGSC) is considered as the most common and deadliest
ovarian cancer subtype with poor prognosis. Currently available standard therapies do not eﬀec-
tively improve patient survival, and due to the immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment (TME)
patients also respond poorly to immunotherapies. To understand these immunosuppressive mech-
anisms and to develop novel therapeutic strategies, it is crucial to understand the dynamic and
complex interactions between cancer cells and their microenvironment. One of the key approaches
to decode these cell-cell communication patterns is to study ligand receptor interactions (LRIs)
by analysing their respective gene expressions with their spatial context. Among many spatial
transcriptomics methods, Geomx has recently gained popularity among scientists as a minimally
invasive high-plex technique which allows ﬂexible selection of areas of interest within the TME.

mics methods, Geomx has recently gained popularity among scientists as a minimally
invasive high-plex technique which allows ﬂexible selection of areas of interest within the TME.
However, there are currently no tools speciﬁcally available to predict ligands and their receptors
from these spatial data.
This thesis primarily focuses on developing a pipeline to predict and prioritize LRIs from spatial
transcriptomics data generated by the GeoMx Digital Spatial Proﬁler (DSP) adopting three widely
used tools ; BulkSignalR(designed for bulk RNAseq data), CellChat and NicheNet (both designed
for scRNAseq data). The pipeline is then used to predict LRIs within the stromal and tumor areas
of interest to identify spatially distinct communication between immune cells speciﬁcally focusing
on T Cells and Macrophages. From the predictions, we were able to prioritize 22 LR pairs based
on the LRI scores, statistical signiﬁcance and expression regulation of LR genes, and among them
were well-established therapeutic LR pairs such as CD80/86-CTLA4 , PDCD1LG2-PDCD1. In
conclusion, the analysis showed that the pipeline is able to consistently predict LR pairs with
potential biological signiﬁcance from GeoMx spatial transcriptomics data.
